8. All the people arose as one man, saying, “None of us will go to his tent, neither will any of us turn to his house.
9. But now this is the thing which we will do to Gibeah: we will go up against it by lot;
10. and we will take ten men of one hundred throughout all the tribes of Israel, and one hundred of one thousand, and a thousand out of ten thousand, to get food for the people, that they may do, when they come to Gibeah of Benjamin, according to all the folly that they have worked in Israel.”
11. So all the men of Israel were gathered against the city, knit together as one man.
12. The tribes of Israel sent men through all the tribe of Benjamin, saying, “What wickedness is this that has happened among you?
13. Now therefore deliver up the men, the wicked fellows, who are in Gibeah, that we may put them to death, and put away evil from Israel.” But Benjamin would not listen to the voice of their brothers the children of Israel.
14. The children of Benjamin gathered themselves together out of the cities to Gibeah, to go out to battle against the children of Israel.
15. The children of Benjamin were counted on that day out of the cities twenty-six thousand men who drew the sword, besides the inhabitants of Gibeah, who were counted seven hundred chosen men.
16. Among all these soldiers there were seven hundred chosen men who were left-handed. Every one of them could sling a stone at a hair and not miss.
17. The men of Israel, besides Benjamin, were counted four hundred thousand men who drew sword. All these were men of war.
18. The children of Israel arose, went up to Bethel, and asked counsel of God. They asked, “Who shall go up for us first to battle against the children of Benjamin?” Yahweh said, “Judah first.”
19. The children of Israel rose up in the morning, and encamped against Gibeah.
20. The men of Israel went out to battle against Benjamin; and the men of Israel set the battle in array against them at Gibeah.
21. The children of Benjamin came out of Gibeah, and on that day destroyed twenty-two thousand of the Israelite men down to the ground.
22. The people, the men of Israel, encouraged themselves, and set the battle again in array in the place where they set themselves in array the first day.
23. The children of Israel went up and wept before Yahweh until evening; and they asked of Yahweh, saying, “Shall I again draw near to battle against the children of Benjamin my brother?” Yahweh said, “Go up against him.”
24. The children of Israel came near against the children of Benjamin the second day.
25. Benjamin went out against them out of Gibeah the second day, and destroyed down to the ground of the children of Israel again eighteen thousand men. All these drew the sword.
26. Then all the children of Israel, and all the people, went up, and came to Bethel, and wept, and sat there before Yahweh, and fasted that day until evening; then they offered burnt offerings and peace offerings before Yahweh.
27. The children of Israel asked Yahweh (for the ark of the covenant of God was there in those days,
28. and Phinehas, the son of Eleazar, the son of Aaron, stood before it in those days), saying, “Shall I yet again go out to battle against the children of Benjamin my brother, or shall I cease?” Yahweh said, “Go up; for tomorrow I will deliver him into your hand.”
29. Israel set ambushes all around Gibeah.
30. The children of Israel went up against the children of Benjamin on the third day, and set themselves in array against Gibeah, as at other times.
31. The children of Benjamin went out against the people, and were drawn away from the city; and they began to strike and kill of the people, as at other times, in the highways, of which one goes up to Bethel, and the other to Gibeah, in the field, about thirty men of Israel.
32. The children of Benjamin said, “They are struck down before us, as at the first.” But the children of Israel said, “Let us flee, and draw them away from the city to the highways.”
33. All the men of Israel rose up out of their place, and set themselves in array at Baal Tamar. Then the ambushers of Israel broke out of their place, even out of Maareh Geba.
34. Ten thousand chosen men out of all Israel came over against Gibeah, and the battle was severe; but they didn’t know that disaster was close to them.
35. Yahweh struck Benjamin before Israel; and the children of Israel destroyed of Benjamin that day twenty-five thousand one hundred men. All these drew the sword.
36. So the children of Benjamin saw that they were struck; for the men of Israel yielded to Benjamin, because they trusted the ambushers whom they had set against Gibeah.
37. The ambushers hurried, and rushed on Gibeah; then the ambushers spread out, and struck all the city with the edge of the sword.
38. Now the appointed sign between the men of Israel and the ambushers was that they should make a great cloud of smoke rise up out of the city.
39. The men of Israel turned in the battle, and Benjamin began to strike and kill of the men of Israel about thirty persons; for they said, “Surely they are struck down before us, as in the first battle.”
40. But when the cloud began to arise up out of the city in a pillar of smoke, the Benjamites looked behind them; and behold, the whole city went up in smoke to the sky.
41. The men of Israel turned, and the men of Benjamin were dismayed; for they saw that disaster had come on them.
42. Therefore they turned their backs before the men of Israel to the way of the wilderness; but the battle followed hard after them; and those who came out of the cities destroyed them in the middle of it.
43. They surrounded the Benjamites, chased them, and trod them down at their resting place, as far as near Gibeah toward the sunrise.
44. Eighteen thousand men of Benjamin fell; all these were men of valor.
45. They turned and fled toward the wilderness to the rock of Rimmon; and they gleaned five thousand men of them in the highways, and followed hard after them to Gidom, and struck two thousand men of them.
46. So that all who fell that day of Benjamin were twenty-five thousand men who drew the sword. All these were men of valor.
47. But six hundred men turned and fled toward the wilderness to the rock of Rimmon, and stayed in the rock of Rimmon four months.
48. The men of Israel turned again on the children of Benjamin, and struck them with the edge of the sword, both the entire city, and the livestock, and all that they found. Moreover all the cities which they found they set on fire.
20.notes:
Judges - Chapter 20
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
All Israel unites against Benjamin for the atrocity at Gibeah, and after two devastating defeats, God gives them victory on the third day. Benjamin is nearly wiped out—only 600 men survive, fleeing to the rock of Rimmon.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Unknown, traditionally attributed to Samuel. Judges covers
approximately 350 years (1380-1050 BC) between Joshua's death and the
rise of the monarchy. Hebrew title: 'Shophetim' — 'Judges' (deliverers/
rulers raised by God). The book records Israel's repeated cycle: sin →
oppression → crying out → deliverance → rest → sin again. The refrain:
'In those days there was no king in Israel: every man did that which was
right in his own eyes' (17:6, 21:25). The book demonstrates humanity's
need for a righteous king — ultimately fulfilled in Christ.

Historical Context: Chapter 20 records the civil war that erupted in
response to the atrocity at Gibeah (chapter 19). All Israel assembles —
"from Dan even to Beer-sheba" (v.1) — 400,000 armed men united against
Benjamin's refusal to surrender the guilty men of Gibeah. This is the
only time in the Judges period that all Israel acts in concert, and
ironically it is not against a foreign oppressor but against one of their
own tribes. The chapter presents a complex theological narrative: Israel
is right to demand justice for the crime at Gibeah, yet their execution
of that justice is deeply flawed. They inquire of God, yet their first
two attacks result in devastating defeats — 22,000 dead on the first day,
18,000 on the second. Only after fasting, weeping, and offering
sacrifices does God grant them victory on the third day. The question
arises: why did God allow Israel to suffer 40,000 casualties while
pursuing justice? Several answers emerge. First, Israel's own sin needed
purging — they were not morally superior to Benjamin. Second, their
initial inquiry of God was superficial: "Which of us shall go up first?"
(v.18) — they asked about tactics, not whether they should fight at all.
Third, the escalating devotion (weeping in v.23, then fasting and
sacrifices in v.26) suggests God was driving them to deeper dependence.
The presence of Phinehas son of Eleazar (v.28) — Aaron's grandson —
places this event early in the Judges period, despite its position at
the end of the book. The military strategy of the third day mirrors
Joshua's conquest of Ai (Joshua 8): a feigned retreat drawing the enemy
out, followed by an ambush and the burning of the city. The near-
annihilation of Benjamin — from 26,700 warriors to just 600 survivors —
raises the specter of a tribe being erased from Israel entirely, setting
up the crisis of chapter 21.

Israel Assembles at Mizpah (vv.1-11): "All the children of Israel went
out, and the congregation was gathered together as one man, from Dan even
to Beer-sheba" (v.1). This phrase — "from Dan to Beer-sheba" — describes
the full extent of Israel, north to south. The assembly at Mizpah
includes 400,000 armed men. The Levite presents his case (vv.4-7),
though notably he omits his own role in pushing his concubine outside.
He claims the men "thought to have slain me" (v.5) — emphasizing the
threat to himself rather than the actual crime against her. His account
is self-serving, yet the underlying crime is real. Israel responds
unanimously: "We will not any of us go to his tent, neither will we any
of us turn into his house" (v.8) — they will not rest until justice is
done. They send messengers to Benjamin demanding the surrender of "the
children of Belial, which are in Gibeah, that we may put them to death,
and put away evil from Israel" (v.13). This demand is consistent with
Deuteronomy 13:12-15 and 17:12 — the requirement to purge evil from the
community. But Benjamin refuses. Tribal loyalty overrides moral duty.
They choose to protect their guilty rather than submit to justice.

Benjamin's Defiance and Military Strength (vv.12-17): Benjamin musters
26,000 swordsmen plus 700 chosen men from Gibeah itself. Among them are
700 left-handed slingers who "could sling stones at an hair breadth, and
not miss" (v.16). This detail recalls Ehud, the left-handed Benjamite
judge (Judges 3:15). Benjamin was small but fierce — their warriors were
elite. Israel's 400,000 against Benjamin's 26,700 represents roughly a
15-to-1 advantage, yet the first two battles go to Benjamin. Numbers
alone do not determine outcomes.

The First Two Defeats (vv.18-25): Israel inquires of God: "Which of us
shall go up first?" (v.18). God answers: "Judah shall go up first" —
the same answer given at the book's opening (Judges 1:2). But the first
attack fails catastrophically: Benjamin kills 22,000 Israelites. Israel
"encouraged themselves" (v.22) and asks again: "Shall I go up again to
battle against the children of Benjamin my brother?" (v.23). God says
"Go up against him." The second day: 18,000 more Israelites fall. Total
losses: 40,000 men — nearly 10% of their army. Why does God permit this?
The text does not explain directly, but the progression of Israel's
response is instructive. After the first defeat: they "encouraged
themselves" (v.22) — self-reliance. After the second: they "went up, and
came unto the house of God, and wept, and sat there before the LORD, and
fasted that day until even, and offered burnt offerings and peace
offerings" (v.26). The defeats drive them from self-confidence to genuine
dependence on God.

The Third Day — Victory Through Ambush (vv.26-48): After fasting and
sacrifice, Israel inquires again — but now with a different posture:
"Shall I yet again go out to battle against the children of Benjamin my
brother, or shall I cease?" (v.28). They are now willing to stop if God
says stop. This is the posture God was waiting for. His answer: "Go up;
for to morrow I will deliver them into thine hand" (v.28). The strategy
mirrors Ai (Joshua 8): Israel sets an ambush, feigns retreat to draw
Benjamin out of the city, then the ambush force strikes Gibeah and burns
it. The signal — "a great flame with smoke rise up out of the city"
(v.38) — tells the retreating Israelites to turn and fight. Benjamin,
seeing the smoke, realizes "evil was come upon them" (v.41). The rout is
total: 25,100 Benjamites fall. The survivors flee to the rock of Rimmon
— only 600 men escape. Israel then systematically destroys every
Benjamite city, killing men, women, children, and livestock (v.48). The
punishment, while responding to genuine evil, becomes excessive —
approaching genocide of an entire tribe.

The Theological Complexity: This chapter resists simple moral readings.
Israel is right to demand justice. Benjamin is wrong to protect the
guilty. Yet Israel's execution of justice becomes disproportionate
destruction. God authorizes the battle but allows Israel to suffer first
— purging their self-righteousness through defeat before granting
victory. The 40,000 Israelite dead remind us that pursuing justice in a
fallen world always costs something. And the near-extinction of Benjamin
shows that righteous anger, unchecked by mercy, can become its own form
of evil.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Mizpah: Assembly point for the eleven tribes (likely Mizpah in Benjamin, north of Jerusalem)
- Gibeah (Benjamin): The target city — located on the central ridge road between Jerusalem and Ramah
- Bethel: Where Israel inquires of God (the ark is there); the battle takes place in the narrow passes around Gibeah

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | 1 Samuel    | 7       | 5-6                | Israel later assembles at Mizpah under Samuel.
v.1              | Judges      | 11      | 11                 | Mizpah as a place of assembly "before the LORD."
v.2              | Judges      | 21      | 10                 | The same assembly makes the oath about Jabesh- gilead.
v.13             | Deuteronomy | 13      | 12-15              | The law requiring purging of evil from cities in Israel.
v.16             | Judges      | 3       | 15                 | Ehud the left-handed Benjamite — a tribal trait.
v.18             | Judges      | 1       | 1-2                | "Who shall go up first?" — same question, same answer (Judah), at the book's beginning.
v.23             | 2 Chronicles | 20      | 15-17              | "The battle is not yours but God's" (ESV) — the principle Israel had to learn.
v.26             | Joel        | 2       | 12-13              | "Return to me with all your heart, with fasting, with weeping." (ESV)
v.27             | 1 Samuel    | 4       | 3-4                | The Ark of the Covenant at Shiloh/Bethel.
v.28             | Numbers     | 25      | 7-13               | Phinehas' zeal at Baal-peor — his presence dates this event early in the Judges period.
v.29             | Joshua      | 8       | 2-22               | The ambush strategy at Ai — the same tactic used against Gibeah.
v.35             | Psalm       | 44      | 3                  | "For not by their own sword did they win the land... but your right hand." (ESV)
v.47             | 1 Samuel    | 14      | 2                  | The rock of Rimmon near Gibeah — Saul later sits under a pomegranate tree there.
v.48             | Deuteronomy | 20      | 16-18              | Laws of total warfare — applied here against fellow Israelites.

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/judges-20/
  Guzik emphasizes that Israel's initial defeats were God's discipline
  for their own sin — they were not morally superior to Benjamin. He
  notes the progression from casual inquiry to genuine brokenness, and
  that God required humility before granting victory.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "Forty thousand men of Israel fell before God gave the victory. Why?
  Because God will not be used as a rubber stamp for human plans. They
  asked 'Who shall go first?' — a question of strategy. They should have
  asked 'Are we right with Thee?' God sometimes lets His people fail in
  a righteous cause until they learn that the cause alone does not
  guarantee success. Right doctrine with wrong hearts still falls before
  the enemy. Only when they wept, fasted, and offered sacrifice — only
  when they were broken — did God say, 'Tomorrow I will deliver them.'"

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "from Dan even to Beer-sheba" / ESV: "from Dan to Beersheba" — Hebrew "mi-Dan v'ad Be'er-sheva." A merism (figure of speech using two extremes to mean "everything between") for the entire nation. Dan is the northernmost city; Beer-sheba the southernmost. This phrase becomes standard for "all Israel" (1 Samuel 3:20; 2 Samuel 3:10; 1 Kings 4:25).
v.2              | KJV         | "the chief of all the people" / ESV: "the chiefs of all the people" — Hebrew "pinnot kol-ha'am." "Pinnot" = corners, cornerstones. Metaphorically: the chief men, the "cornerstones" of the people — those who hold the community together. The image is architectural: leaders as the structural supports of society.
v.16             | KJV         | "could sling stones at an hair breadth, and not miss" / ESV: "could sling a stone at a hair and not miss" — Hebrew "el-hassa'arah v'lo yachati." "Sa'arah" = a hair. "Chata" = to miss (the same root as "sin" — chata'ah — which literally means "missing the mark"). These slingers never "sinned" — never missed their target. The wordplay is intentional: Benjamin's warriors never miss with stones, yet the tribe has catastrophically missed the moral mark.
v.26             | KJV         | "went up, and came unto the house of God" / ESV: "went up and came to Bethel" — Hebrew "vaya'alu Beit-El." "Beit-El" can mean "house of God" (a description of any worship site) or "Bethel" (the specific city). KJV translates it descriptively; ESV treats it as a proper noun. Both are linguistically valid. The Ark's presence (v.27) suggests this is the actual location of the tabernacle or a legitimate worship site.
v.33             | KJV         | "out of the meadows of Gibeah" / ESV: "from Maareh-geba" — Hebrew "mimma'areh-gava." KJV reads this as "from the meadow/ open place of Gibeah." ESV transliterates it as a place name "Maareh-geba." The Hebrew is uncertain — "ma'areh" could derive from "arah" (to be bare/open) or be a proper noun. The location is the staging area for the ambush force.
v.43             | KJV         | "trode them down with ease over against Gibeah toward the sunrising" / ESV: "trod them down from Nohah as far as opposite Gibeah on the east" — Hebrew "hiddrikhuhu m'nuchah ad-nekhach hagiv'ah." KJV reads "m'nuchah" as "with ease/rest" (from "menucha"). ESV reads it as "from Nohah" (a place name, cf. 1 Chronicles 8:2). The textual difficulty affects whether this describes the manner of pursuit or its geographic path.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Mizpah            | Hebrew "mitspah" = watchtower/lookout. A city in Benjamin where Israel frequently assembled for national decisions (Judges 20:1; 1 Samuel 7:5; 10:17). Its elevated position made it suitable for large gatherings. Not to be confused with other Mizpahs in Gilead or Moab. | 
Phinehas          | Son of Eleazar, grandson of Aaron the high priest. His presence serving before the Ark (v.28) dates this event to the first generation after the conquest — early in the Judges period. Known for his zeal at Baal-peor (Numbers 25:7-13), where God granted him a covenant of perpetual priesthood. | 
Rock of Rimmon    | A rocky stronghold near Gibeah where 600 Benjamite survivors took refuge for four months. "Rimmon" = pomegranate. The site provided natural defensive advantages that allowed the remnant to survive Israel's pursuit. Likely modern Rammun, about 4 miles east of Bethel. | 
Liers in Wait     | KJV term for an ambush force. Hebrew "orvim" from "arav" = to lie in wait, to ambush. The same military tactic used at Ai (Joshua 8). A concealed force that attacks from an unexpected direction while the main army draws the enemy out. | KJV term
Baal              | tamar     — "Lord/master of the palm tree." The staging point for Israel's main force on the third day of battle. The exact location is unknown but was near Gibeah. The name's pagan element ("Baal") may reflect Canaanite influence on local place names. | 
Sons of Belial    | See Judges 19 glossary. Hebrew "b'nei v'liyya'al." The worthless, wicked men of Gibeah whose crime triggered the entire civil war. Benjamin's refusal to surrender them made the entire tribe complicit in their guilt. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Right cause does not guarantee right outcome without right hearts
   (vv.18-25). Israel pursued justice — a righteous cause. Yet they
   suffered 40,000 casualties before victory. Having the right position is
   not enough; God requires the right posture. Self-righteous crusaders
   often fail even when their cause is just, because God will not bless
   pride even in pursuit of justice.

2. God uses defeat to produce dependence (vv.22-26). The progression is
   clear: after the first defeat, Israel "encouraged themselves" (self-
   reliance). After the second, they wept, fasted, and sacrificed
   (God-reliance). Sometimes God allows us to fail — even in righteous
   endeavors — to break our self-sufficiency and drive us to genuine
   dependence on Him.

3. Protecting the guilty implicates the protectors (v.13). Benjamin's
   refusal to surrender the men of Gibeah made the entire tribe complicit.
   When communities, institutions, or families protect wrongdoers from
   accountability, they share in the guilt. Loyalty that shields evil is
   not virtue — it is complicity.

4. The cost of justice in a fallen world (vv.21,25,35). Pursuing
   righteousness cost Israel 40,000 men before it cost Benjamin 25,000.
   Justice is never free. Standing against evil always exacts a price.
   Those who think righteousness should be easy or costless have not
   understood the nature of the battle.

5. Righteous anger without mercy becomes its own evil (v.48). Israel's
   response to Gibeah's sin — destroying every Benjamite city, killing
   women and children — exceeds proportional justice. Vengeance that knows
   no restraint becomes indistinguishable from the evil it punishes. Even
   justified wrath must be tempered by the recognition that we too are
   sinners in need of mercy.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Judges Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/judges/

David Guzik — Judges 20 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/judges-20/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

21.ESV:
1. Now the men of Israel had sworn at Mizpah, “No one of us shall give his daughter in marriage to Benjamin.”
2. And the people came to Bethel and sat there till evening before God, and they lifted up their voices and wept bitterly.
3. And they said, “O LORD, the God of Israel, why has this happened in Israel, that today there should be one tribe lacking in Israel?”
4. And the next day the people rose early and built there an altar and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings.
5. And the people of Israel said, “Which of all the tribes of Israel did not come up in the assembly to the LORD?” For they had taken a great oath concerning him who did not come up to the LORD to Mizpah, saying, “He shall surely be put to death.”
6. And the people of Israel had compassion for Benjamin their brother and said, “One tribe is cut off from Israel this day.
7. What shall we do for wives for those who are left, since we have sworn by the LORD that we will not give them any of our daughters for wives?”
8. And they said, “What one is there of the tribes of Israel that did not come up to the LORD to Mizpah?” And behold, no one had come to the camp from Jabesh-gilead, to the assembly.
9. For when the people were mustered, behold, not one of the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead was there.
10. So the congregation sent 12,000 of their bravest men there and commanded them, “Go and strike the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead with the edge of the sword; also the women and the little ones.
11. This is what you shall do: every male and every woman that has lain with a male you shall devote to destruction.”
12. And they found among the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead 400 young virgins who had not known a man by lying with him, and they brought them to the camp at Shiloh, which is in the land of Canaan.
13. Then the whole congregation sent word to the people of Benjamin who were at the rock of Rimmon and proclaimed peace to them.
14. And Benjamin returned at that time. And they gave them the women whom they had saved alive of the women of Jabesh-gilead, but they were not enough for them.
15. And the people had compassion on Benjamin because the LORD had made a breach in the tribes of Israel.
16. Then the elders of the congregation said, “What shall we do for wives for those who are left, since the women are destroyed out of Benjamin?”
17. And they said, “There must be an inheritance for the survivors of Benjamin, that a tribe not be blotted out from Israel.
18. Yet we cannot give them wives from our daughters.” For the people of Israel had sworn, “Cursed be he who gives a wife to Benjamin.”
19. So they said, “Behold, there is the yearly feast of the LORD at Shiloh, which is north of Bethel, on the east of the highway that goes up from Bethel to Shechem, and south of Lebonah.”
20. And they commanded the people of Benjamin, saying, “Go and lie in ambush in the vineyards
21. and watch. If the daughters of Shiloh come out to dance in the dances, then come out of the vineyards and snatch each man his wife from the daughters of Shiloh, and go to the land of Benjamin.
22. And when their fathers or their brothers come to complain to us, we will say to them, ‘Grant them graciously to us, because we did not take for each man of them his wife in battle, neither did you give them to them, else you would now be guilty.’”
23. And the people of Benjamin did so and took their wives, according to their number, from the dancers whom they carried off. Then they went and returned to their inheritance and rebuilt the towns and lived in them.
24. And the people of Israel departed from there at that time, every man to his tribe and family, and they went out from there every man to his inheritance.
25. In those days there was no king in Israel. Everyone did what was right in his own eyes.
21.KJV:
1. Now the men of Israel had sworn in Mizpeh, saying, There shall not any of us give his daughter unto Benjamin to wife.
2. And the people came to the house of God, and abode there till even before God, and lifted up their voices, and wept sore;
3. And said, O LORD God of Israel, why is this come to pass in Israel, that there should be to day one tribe lacking in Israel?
4. And it came to pass on the morrow, that the people rose early, and built there an altar, and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings.
5. And the children of Israel said, Who is there among all the tribes of Israel that came not up with the congregation unto the LORD? For they had made a great oath concerning him that came not up to the LORD to Mizpeh, saying, He shall surely be put to death.
6. And the children of Israel repented them for Benjamin their brother, and said, There is one tribe cut off from Israel this day.
7. How shall we do for wives for them that remain, seeing we have sworn by the LORD that we will not give them of our daughters to wives?
8. And they said, What one is there of the tribes of Israel that came not up to Mizpeh to the LORD? And, behold, there came none to the camp from Jabesh-gilead to the assembly.
9. For the people were numbered, and, behold, there were none of the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead there.
10. And the congregation sent thither twelve thousand men of the valiantest, and commanded them, saying, Go and smite the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead with the edge of the sword, with the women and the children.
11. And this is the thing that ye shall do, Ye shall utterly destroy every male, and every woman that hath lain by man.
12. And they found among the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead four hundred young virgins, that had known no man by lying with any male: and they brought them unto the camp to Shiloh, which is in the land of Canaan.
13. And the whole congregation sent some to speak to the children of Benjamin that were in the rock Rimmon, and to call peaceably unto them.
14. And Benjamin came again at that time; and they gave them wives which they had saved alive of the women of Jabesh-gilead: and yet so they sufficed them not.
15. And the people repented them for Benjamin, because that the LORD had made a breach in the tribes of Israel.
16. Then the elders of the congregation said, How shall we do for wives for them that remain, seeing the women are destroyed out of Benjamin?
17. And they said, There must be an inheritance for them that be escaped of Benjamin, that a tribe be not destroyed out of Israel.
18. Howbeit we may not give them wives of our daughters: for the children of Israel have sworn, saying, Cursed be he that giveth a wife to Benjamin.
19. Then they said, Behold, there is a feast of the LORD in Shiloh yearly in a place which is on the north side of Bethel, on the east side of the highway that goeth up from Bethel to Shechem, and on the south of Lebonah.
20. Therefore they commanded the children of Benjamin, saying, Go and lie in wait in the vineyards;
21. And see, and, behold, if the daughters of Shiloh come out to dance in dances, then come ye out of the vineyards, and catch you every man his wife of the daughters of Shiloh, and go to the land of Benjamin.
22. And it shall be, when their fathers or their brethren come unto us to complain, that we will say unto them, Be favourable unto them for our sakes: because we reserved not to each man his wife in the war: for ye did not give unto them at this time, that ye should be guilty.
23. And the children of Benjamin did so, and took them wives, according to their number, of them that danced, whom they caught: and they went and returned unto their inheritance, and repaired the cities, and dwelt in them.
24. And the children of Israel departed thence at that time, every man to his tribe and to his family, and they went out from thence every man to his inheritance.
25. In those days there was no king in Israel: every man did that which was right in his own eyes.

22. And it shall be, when their fathers or their brethren come to complain unto us, that we will say unto them, Grant them graciously unto us, because we took not for each man [of them] his wife in battle, neither did ye give them unto them, else would ye now be guilty.
23. And the children of Benjamin did so, and took them wives, according to their number, of them that danced, whom they carried off: and they went and returned unto their inheritance, and built the cities, and dwelt in them.

21.NET:
1. The Israelites had taken an oath in Mizpah, saying, “Not one of us will allow his daughter to marry a Benjaminite.”
2. So the people came to Bethel and sat there before God until evening, weeping loudly and uncontrollably.
3. They said, “Why, O Lord God of Israel, has this happened in Israel? An entire tribe has disappeared from Israel today!”

21.notes:
Judges - Chapter 21
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Israel grieves over the near-extinction of Benjamin and devises ways to provide wives for the 600 survivors without breaking their oath. The book ends: "In those days there was no king in Israel: every man did that which was right in his own eyes."

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Unknown, traditionally attributed to Samuel. Judges covers
approximately 350 years (1380-1050 BC) between Joshua's death and the
rise of the monarchy. Hebrew title: 'Shophetim' — 'Judges' (deliverers/
rulers raised by God). The book records Israel's repeated cycle: sin →
oppression → crying out → deliverance → rest → sin again. The refrain:
'In those days there was no king in Israel: every man did that which was
right in his own eyes' (17:6, 21:25). The book demonstrates humanity's
need for a righteous king — ultimately fulfilled in Christ.

Historical Context: Chapter 21 is the final chapter of Judges and
concludes both the Gibeah narrative (chapters 19-21) and the entire book.
It records Israel's desperate — and morally bankrupt — attempts to
provide wives for the 600 surviving Benjamite men at the rock of Rimmon.
The chapter reveals the cascading consequences of rash oaths and
unchecked violence. Israel had sworn two oaths at Mizpah: (1) no one
would give their daughter in marriage to Benjamin (v.1), and (2) anyone
who failed to assemble at Mizpah would be put to death (v.5). Now,
having nearly annihilated an entire tribe, they weep over the
consequences of their own actions. Their "solution" involves two further
atrocities: destroying Jabesh-gilead (killing all men, married women,
and children) to obtain 400 virgins for Benjamin, and then authorizing
the abduction of young women dancing at the feast of Shiloh to supply
the remaining 200 wives needed. The chapter is a masterpiece of dark
irony: Israel began by seeking justice for the rape of one woman (chapter
19) and ends by orchestrating the mass abduction of women for forced
marriage. They have become what they condemned. The legal casuistry they
employ — technically they did not "give" their daughters since the women
were "taken" — reveals the moral bankruptcy of a people who manipulate
the letter of the law while violating its spirit. The book's final verse
delivers the author's verdict on the entire period: "In those days there
was no king in Israel: every man did that which was right in his own
eyes" (v.25). This is not merely a political observation — it is a
theological indictment. Without righteous authority, even well-
intentioned people produce chaos, violence, and injustice. The book ends
not with resolution but with a cry for the king who will bring order out
of moral anarchy — pointing forward to David and ultimately to Christ.

Israel's Grief and the Oath Problem (vv.1-7): The men of Israel had
sworn at Mizpah: "There shall not any of us give his daughter unto
Benjamin to wife" (v.1). Now, with only 600 Benjamite men surviving and
all Benjamite women dead (20:48), the tribe faces extinction. Israel
comes to Bethel (the house of God), weeps until evening, and cries out:
"O LORD God of Israel, why is this come to pass in Israel, that there
should be to day one tribe lacking in Israel?" (v.3). The question is
remarkable in its lack of self-awareness — they themselves caused this
through their excessive violence. They built an altar and offered
sacrifices (v.4), seeking God's guidance. But their "solution" will not
come from God — it will come from human scheming that produces more
violence. The oath creates an impossible situation: they cannot give
wives to Benjamin without breaking their vow, yet without wives Benjamin
will perish. Rather than acknowledging their oath was rash and seeking
God's release from it, they seek loopholes.

The Destruction of Jabesh-gilead (vv.8-14): Israel discovers that no one
from Jabesh-gilead attended the assembly at Mizpah. The second oath
activates: those who did not come must die. They send 12,000 warriors
with orders to "smite the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead with the edge of
the sword, with the women and the children" (v.10) — sparing only
virgin women. They find 400 young virgins and bring them to Shiloh. The
600 Benjamites are called from the rock of Rimmon and offered peace.
The 400 women are given to them — "and yet so they sufficed them not"
(v.14). Two hundred men remain without wives. The moral calculus is
staggering: to preserve one tribe, they destroy an entire city. To
provide wives for men who defended rapists, they kill families and take
their daughters. The "solution" replicates the very evil that started
the crisis — violence against the vulnerable to serve the interests of
men.

The Abduction at Shiloh (vv.15-23): Still 200 wives short, the elders
devise another scheme. They identify the annual feast of the LORD at
Shiloh — a legitimate religious festival — and instruct the Benjamites:
"Go and lie in wait in the vineyards; and see, and, behold, if the
daughters of Shiloh come out to dance in dances, then come ye out of the
vineyards, and catch you every man his wife" (vv.20-21). The plan
includes a prepared excuse for the fathers and brothers who will
complain: "Be favourable unto them for our sakes: because we reserved
not to each man his wife in the war: for ye did not give unto them at
this time, that ye should be guilty" (v.22). The legal fiction is
transparent: since the women were "seized" rather than "given," the oath
is technically unbroken. This is casuistry at its worst — the letter of
the law preserved while its spirit is demolished. Young women at worship
are abducted into forced marriages, and the elders of Israel authorize
it. The feast of the LORD — meant for worship and joy — becomes the
setting for organized kidnapping.

The Book's Conclusion (vv.23-25): Benjamin takes wives, returns to their
inheritance, and rebuilds their cities. Israel disperses — "every man to
his tribe and to his family" (v.24). Then the final verdict: "In those
days there was no king in Israel: every man did that which was right in
his own eyes" (v.25). This closing statement frames the entire book. It
appeared first in 17:6, introducing the appendix. Now it closes the
book. The repetition is deliberate: everything recorded in chapters 17-21
— Micah's idolatry, Dan's apostasy, the rape at Gibeah, the civil war,
the destruction of Jabesh-gilead, the abduction at Shiloh — all of it
flows from the absence of righteous authority. "Right in his own eyes"
is the author's diagnosis of the human condition without God's rule. It
is not freedom — it is chaos. It is not autonomy — it is moral anarchy.
The book of Judges ends with a question hanging in the air: Who will be
the king that Israel needs? The answer unfolds through Ruth, Samuel, and
ultimately David — and beyond David to David's greater Son.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Jabesh-gilead: A city in Transjordan (Gilead) that did not join the assembly — its unmarried women are taken for Benjamin
- Shiloh: Where the remaining Benjamite men seize wives during the annual feast/dance in the vineyards
- The remnant of Benjamin: 600 survivors hiding at the rock of Rimmon in the wilderness east of Bethel

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Judges      | 11      | 30-40              | Jephthah's rash vow — another oath with devastating consequences in Judges.
v.2              | Judges      | 20      | 26-27              | The same pattern: weeping before God at Bethel.
v.3              | Joshua      | 7       | 7                  | Joshua's similar cry: "Alas, O Lord GOD, why have you brought this people over the Jordan at all?" (ESV)
v.5              | Judges      | 5       | 23                 | "Curse Meroz, says the angel of the LORD, curse its inhabitants thoroughly, because they did not come to the help of the LORD" (ESV) — precedent for punishing non-participation.
v.8              | 1 Samuel    | 11      | 1-11               | Jabesh-gilead later rescued by Saul from the Ammonites — the city remembers Benjamin's connection.
v.8              | 1 Samuel    | 31      | 11-13              | Men of Jabesh-gilead retrieve Saul's body — loyalty to Benjamin persists across generations.
v.12             | Joshua      | 18      | 1                  | Shiloh as the location of the tabernacle.
v.15             | Romans      | 9       | 27                 | "Though the number of the sons of Israel be as the sand of the sea, only a remnant of them will be saved." (ESV)
v.19             | Exodus      | 23      | 14-17              | The three annual feasts required of Israel.
v.21             | Deuteronomy | 23      | 21-23              | Laws regarding vows — once made, they must be kept.
v.25             | Judges      | 17      | 6                  | The first occurrence of this refrain, introducing the appendix.
v.25             | 1 Samuel    | 8       | 5-6                | Israel's eventual demand for a king — the answer to Judges' crisis.
v.25             | Proverbs    | 14      | 12                 | "There is a way that seems right to a man, but its end is the way to death." (ESV)
v.25             | Deuteronomy | 12      | 8                  | "You shall not do according to all that we are doing here today, everyone doing whatever is right in his own eyes." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/judges-21/
  Guzik emphasizes the tragic irony: Israel's "solution" to the crisis
  they created involves more violence and the very abuse of women that
  triggered the war. He notes that the book ends without resolution —
  deliberately pointing forward to the need for a righteous king.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "Every man did that which was right in his own eyes.' Here is the
  epitaph of a nation in ruins. When every man becomes his own god, his
  own lawgiver, his own standard of right and wrong — then there is no
  right at all, only competing selfishnesses tearing the fabric of
  society to pieces. The book of Judges does not end with a period but
  with a question mark: Where is the King? And the answer echoes down
  the centuries until a child is born in Bethlehem of whom it is said,
  'He shall reign over the house of Jacob for ever.'"

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "came to the house of God" / ESV: "came to Bethel" — Hebrew "Beit-El." Same ambiguity as in 20:26. KJV translates descriptively ("house of God"); ESV uses the proper noun ("Bethel"). Both are valid renderings of the same Hebrew. The context (altar-building, sacrifices) suggests a legitimate worship site, whether Bethel the city or the tabernacle location.
v.6              | KJV         | "repented them for Benjamin" / ESV: "had compassion for Benjamin" — Hebrew "vayinnachamu." From "nacham" = to be sorry, to comfort oneself, to relent. KJV's "repented" uses the older English sense of "felt regret/sorrow" (not moral repentance). ESV's "had compassion" captures the emotional dimension. The Hebrew conveys grief over consequences — they are sorry for what happened, though not necessarily for their role in causing it.
v.11             | KJV         | "Ye shall utterly destroy every male" / ESV: "every male and every woman that has lain with a male you shall devote to destruction" — Hebrew "tachariymu." From "charam" = to devote to destruction, to place under the ban. This is the same "herem" language used for the Canaanite conquest (Joshua 6:17-21). Its application to a fellow Israelite city is extraordinary and reveals how far the situation has deteriorated — Israel treats its own people as it was commanded to treat the Canaanites.
v.19             | KJV         | "a feast of the LORD in Shiloh yearly" / ESV: "the yearly feast of the LORD at Shiloh" — Hebrew "chag-Adonai b'Shiloh miyamim yamimah." "Chag" = a pilgrimage feast. "Miyamim yamimah" = literally "from days to days" = annually. This is likely one of the three required annual feasts (Passover, Weeks, or Tabernacles). The dancing suggests Tabernacles (Sukkot), the harvest celebration.
v.21             | KJV         | "catch you every man his wife" / ESV: "snatch each man his wife" — Hebrew "vachataphtem." From "chataph" = to seize, snatch, carry off. The word implies violent seizure — not courtship or consent. It is the language of predation. The same root appears in Job 9:12 ("who can hinder him?") and Psalm 10:9 (the lion snatching prey). The authorized "catching" of women at worship is linguistically indistinguishable from kidnapping.
v.25             | KJV         | "every man did that which was right in his own eyes" / ESV: "Everyone did what was right in his own eyes" — Hebrew "ish hayashar b'einav ya'aseh." "Yashar" = straight, right, upright. "B'einav" = in his eyes. The irony: "yashar" is the word for moral uprightness, yet here it describes moral chaos. Each person defines "right" for themselves — which means there is no objective right at all. The word "yashar" also connects to "Jeshurun" (the upright one) — Israel's ideal name. They claim uprightness while practicing wickedness.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Jabesh            | gilead  — A city in the Transjordan territory of Gilead (modern Jordan). Its failure to attend the Mizpah assembly led to its destruction. Later, Jabesh-gilead develops a special bond with Benjamin/Saul: Saul rescues them from Nahash the Ammonite (1 Samuel 11), and they retrieve his body after his death (1 Samuel 31:11-13). | 
Shiloh            | The central worship site of Israel during the Judges period, where the tabernacle was located (Joshua 18:1). Located in Ephraim, approximately 20 miles north of Jerusalem. The annual feast held there was a legitimate religious observance corrupted by the Benjamite abduction scheme. God later destroyed Shiloh as judgment (Jeremiah 7:12-14). | 
Rash Oath         | A vow made hastily, often in the heat of emotion, without considering consequences. Israel's oath not to give daughters to Benjamin (v.1) and the death oath against non-participants (v.5) are both examples. Cf. Jephthah's vow (Judges 11:30-31), Saul's oath (1 Samuel 14:24). Ecclesiastes 5:2-5 warns against hasty vows before God. | 
Feast of the LORD | Hebrew "chag Adonai." One of the three annual pilgrimage festivals required of all Israelite males (Exodus 23:14-17): Passover/Unleavened Bread (spring), Weeks/Pentecost (early summer), and Tabernacles/Booths (autumn). The dancing at Shiloh suggests the joyful harvest celebration of Tabernacles. | 
Right in his own eyes | Hebrew "hayashar b'einav." The book's defining phrase. It describes moral relativism — each person as their own standard of right and wrong. It is the opposite of Deuteronomy 6:18: "thou shalt do that which is right in the sight of the LORD" (hayashar b'einei Adonai). The contrast: God's eyes vs. human eyes as the standard of righteousness. | 
Rock of Rimmon    | The stronghold where 600 Benjamite survivors hid for four months (20:47). Their emergence and reintegration into Israel (v.13-14) represents the preservation of the tribe — God's providence ensuring that no tribe is permanently lost, despite human excess. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Rash oaths create impossible situations (vv.1,7). Israel swore in anger
   what they could not sustain in wisdom. Vows made in the heat of emotion
   — whether in war, conflict, or religious fervor — often produce
   consequences worse than the problem they addressed. Jesus taught: "Let
   your yes be yes and your no be no" (Matthew 5:37). Better to speak
   carefully than to bind yourself rashly before God.

2. Human "solutions" without God's wisdom multiply evil (vv.10-11,20-21).
   Israel's two schemes — destroying Jabesh-gilead and abducting women at
   Shiloh — solve one problem by creating others. When we act from human
   cleverness rather than divine wisdom, our solutions often generate new
   sins. The pattern: sin → consequence → human scheme → more sin. Only
   God's wisdom breaks the cycle.

3. Legal technicalities cannot mask moral failure (v.22). The elders'
   argument — "you didn't give them, so you're not guilty" — is transparent
   manipulation. God is not fooled by loopholes. He looks at the heart, not
   the technicality. Those who use clever reasoning to justify what they
   know is wrong deceive only themselves.

4. The absence of righteous authority produces chaos, not freedom (v.25).
   "Every man did that which was right in his own eyes" sounds like liberty
   but produces atrocity. True freedom requires a framework of
   righteousness. Without God as King — without submission to His authority
   — human autonomy degenerates into competing selfishnesses that destroy
   community. The book of Judges is the Bible's strongest argument against
   moral relativism.

5. The book ends crying out for a King (v.25). Judges does not resolve — it
   aches. It presents the problem without providing the solution. The
   solution comes in stages: Samuel, then David, then ultimately Christ —
   the righteous King under whose rule "every man doing right in his own
   eyes" is replaced by every knee bowing and every tongue confessing that
   Jesus Christ is Lord (Philippians 2:10-11).

------------------------------------

  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/judges-21/

  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/
